comments.body: one database per user doesn't make any sense. What are you trying to do?
I programming crm system. Its web project. For one company there will be one base, for another another.
Could you provide any code, please, or links for github related to your project?
@shvetsovitaliy, so what's the problem with your code?
@shvetsovitaliy, could you clarify, please, what do you want to get as result? two databases in the same project or what? If two databases, does this project work for you now or not?

Thanks to everyone who wants to help me. My code works fine. The goal is not to create subdomains, but to have one application and many databases. Each company (with a certain count of employees) could go into their databases. This is exactly what I can't realised.  In application.properties I have one database now and many users can connect to it. And what I need: 1 group of people can see one database, another group of people can see another one.

If I understand you right, you're assuming, that each company already have theire own database with company specific users and your application, more specific Spring Security, has to access all of them to authenticate them with your application?! If so, that's a very strange solution. If anything, you should address the identity providers of the companies and not their database directly. For example, use their LDAP, OAuth2 or SAML interface for authentication and, if necessary, synchronize the user data in your own database.

Your answer could be improved with additional supporting information. Please edit to add further details, such as citations or documentation, so that others can confirm that your answer is correct. You can find more information on how to write good answers in the help center.
I wonder who downvoted you, your answer is good.

answers.body:
You can handle multi-database (multi-tenant) login in Spring Boot using a multi-tenant architecture. There are two common approaches:

Database-per-tenant (your case) – each company has its own schema or database.

  • Use a RoutingDataSource (extend AbstractRoutingDataSource) that selects the correct DataSource based on the tenant ID (e.g., company identifier).
  • Resolve the tenant after authentication — for example, from the user’s login info or domain — and store it in a ThreadLocal or TenantContext.
  • Spring Security’s AuthenticationSuccessHandler can set the tenant context right after login. Schema-per-tenant – same approach, but you switch schemas instead of full DataSources.

Best practice: Keep tenant resolution centralized (via a TenantResolver), cache DataSources if needed, and make sure transactions always use the correct tenant context. Spring Boot + Hibernate supports this natively via MultiTenantConnectionProvider and CurrentTenantIdentifierResolver.

public class TenantRoutingDataSource extends AbstractRoutingDataSource {
    @Override
    protected Object determineCurrentLookupKey() {
        return TenantContext.getCurrentTenant();
    }
}

title: Users must log in to different databases in a Java application (MVC, Spring)
body:
Now I'm programming my Java application on Spring (MVC). Need some help with code. this is crm system. Web project. For one company there will be one base, for another another. I used spring boot and spring security for my project.

Thanks for help!

For now I have only one database with some count of users. I want to find some solution of my problem.
